title: Analysis of the Shinji Rei Relationship
display_title: Analysis of the Shinji Rei Relationship
wikitext:
[]: Analysis of the Shinji/Rei Relationship Post by Hiten777 » Fri Oct 30, 2009 12:54 am I know that there have been some threads on this but they are so out of date, about 5 years out of date, so I'm starting a new one courtesy of the new rebuild movies that awakened eva once again. I believe that there is significant evidence of Shinji/Rei relationship that tends to go beyond familial or regular friendship bond, as evidence in the series and manga. I know most have the notion that the manga and series differ and some say that there is no need to read the manga since the anime came out first, but to correctly analyze the characters and thier feelings I think we need to look at both adaptations, since Sadamoto is the character designer and is one of the main people who had a hand in creating her his manga needs to be taken into account. Many people say that Shinji/Rei is not possible, and in an effort not to be biased I will say that although Shinji/Asuka is supposedly the main pairing there are also justifiable hints of Shinji/Rei working just as or better than said pairing. At the start of the series Shinji is shown to be introverted and shy, not wanting to get close to anyone for fear of being hurt. After the whole incident of meeting his father and adjusting to life at Nerv, the first person Shinji is drawn to meeting is Rei, being that she is similar, or in his view "has less" than he does. The beginning shows that Shinji piloted 01 in order to not let Rei get hurt and it is an important scene because it is the first instance that he decides not to run away from his problem, because this time someone's life is on the line. The whole meeting in her apartment then allows Shinji to experience manhood and this focus is then shifted to him wanting to get to know her since they both pilot, but it is not until he hears about how little she values her life that he starts to try and make feel that she's worth something, which begins their friendship after Ramiel. After Asuka's introduction, it is clear that both have come to an understanding about each other. As the series progresses, Rei slowly opens up as an individual, showing genuine concern for Shinji when he's in danger and is even willing to go against her usual self by defending him from Asuka and acting against orders to save him. It is later shown that Rei might have deeper feelings for Shinji as she does more and more things out of character, and the climactic battle with Ramiel proves that she's willing to sacrifice herself to save him from the angel. While some have thought that her saying of "No" after she mentions her desire to become one with Shinji, I believe that one has to assess the situation too. Rei has grown to value Shinji as someone she is close to and we can assume that there might be something deeper to that. When Shinji first went out to save her she grew worried about him and when she saw that teh angel was attacking him, she began to theorize that it was her desire to become one with him, despite claims I think the dub was correct in assessing th situation as not a decline but "You will not" in other words she is saying "I won't let you hurt him too." Does this imply love or romantic interest, vaguely, but to look at her admission about her desire, brought out by the angel it's possible that she did feel something for him. The death of her is also a turning point for Shinji as it bring about the break in his emotions. Turning back to Sadamoto's manga, Rei is given a similar yet new perspective as she is shown to be more human, and it is even more implied that Rei was in love with him. Looking at the manga helps when analyzing the character of both people and is necessary in seeing the in=depth relationship they have.

[]: Bl Post by Alaska Slim » Fri Oct 30, 2009 1:16 am I found one made last year: Depth of Shinji and Rei's relationship

I know most have the notion that the manga and series differ and some say that there is no need to read the manga since the anime came out first, but to correctly analyze the characters and thier feelings I think we need to look at both adaptations, since Sadamoto is the character designer and is one of the main people who had a hand in creating her his manga needs to be taken into account. Many people say that Shinji/Rei is not possible, and in an effort not to be biased I will say that although Shinji/Asuka is supposedly the main pairing there are also justifiable hints of Shinji/Rei working just as or better than said pairing. At the start of the series Shinji is shown to be introverted and shy, not wanting to get close to anyone for fear of being hurt.  each other. As the series progresses, Rei slowly opens up as an individual, showing genuine concern for Shinji when he's in danger and is even willing to go against her usual self by defending him from Asuka and acting against orders to save him. It is later shown that Rei might have deeper feelings for Shinji as she does more and more things out of character, and the climactic battle with Ramiel proves that she's willing to sacrifice herself to save him from the angel. While some have thought that her saying of "No" after she mentions her desire to become one with Shinji, I believe that one has to assess the situation too. Rei has grown to value Shinji as someone she is close to and we can assume that there might be something deeper to that. When Shinji first went out to save her she grew worried about him and when she saw that teh angel was attacking him, she began to theorize that it was her desire to become one with him, despite claims I think the dub was correct in assessing th situation as not a decline but "You will not" in other words she is saying "I won't let you hurt him too." Does this imply love or romantic interest, vaguely, but to look at her admission about her desire, brought out by the angel it's possible that she did feel something for him. The death of her is also a turning point for Shinji as it bring about the break in his emotions. Turning back to Sadamoto's manga, Rei is given a similar yet new perspective as she is shown to be more human, and it is even more implied that Rei was in love with him. Looking at the manga helps when analyzing the character of both people and is necessary in seeing the in=depth relationship they have.
